title: Happy Anniversary

jokes.text: Johnny asks the kids to wish Moira a happy anniversary; David asks 'What would you like us to say?'
jokes.setup: Johnny makes a simple, warm request for the kids to wish their mother a happy anniversary.
jokes.punchline: David responds with a completely literal, emotionally detached question — 'What would you like us to say?' — as if basic human sentiment is a foreign language.
jokes.explanation: A perfect David Rose opener. His complete inability to perform casual familial warmth without explicit instruction is deeply character-specific. The economy is excellent — four words do a lot of work establishing his emotional detachment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Kids, I want you to wish your mother a happy anniversary.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Happy anniversary, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, happy anniversary.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: With a little more enthusiasm, perhaps?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: HAPPY ANNIVERSARY, MOM!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Happy anniversary! We're so glad you married Dad!

jokes.text: Alexis objects to wishing her parents happy anniversary: 'Isn't that something that you two are supposed to say to each other?'
jokes.setup: Both kids are being asked to perform a basic act of familial warmth
jokes.punchline: Alexis reframes it as a logical/procedural question — anniversary wishes are between spouses, therefore they are not obligated — revealing her self-centered emotional logic
jokes.explanation: Alexis's logic is airtight within its own self-serving framework, which is the joke. Slightly less sharp than David's beat because it's a single move rather than an escalation, but still nicely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you guys are celebrating your anniversary? That's so sweet. How many years has it been?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Twenty-seven years.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's amazing. So you guys are basically due for a divorce then, right?

jokes.text: Johnny explains 'it's called being courteous, honey' and brings up the Bloomfield kids throwing anniversary parties.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to teach his kids about basic courtesy by citing the Bloomfield family as a positive example.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately undermines the example — 'the Bloomfields were always a little too friendly with one another' — cutting off Johnny's moral lesson before it lands.
jokes.explanation: A decent two-part joke: Johnny's attempt at teaching propriety gets derailed by a dark insinuation about the Bloomfields. Competent setup-and-redirect humor, elevated slightly by the overlapping dialogue structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The Bloomfields throw anniversary parties every year. It's their thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How... quaint. Though I suppose when you've been together that long, you run out of conversation topics and resort to celebrating the fact that you haven't murdered each other.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, they seem happy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Dad. That's what happens when you marry someone who isn't your cousin.

jokes.text: 'I just don't understand what kind of family skinny dips together.'
jokes.setup: Moira is criticizing the Bloomfields for being 'too friendly' with one another.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'skinny dips together' as her chosen example of inappropriate family closeness — delivered with Moira's patrician horror — is the payoff.
jokes.explanation: The specificity elevates this above a generic 'weird family' joke. Moira's vocal delivery of disgust is clearly implied, and it sets up the 'incestuous Bloomfields' callback moments later.

jokes.text: 'Happy anniversary! Congratulations on your ongoing love for one another. You did it!'
jokes.setup: After being told to say 'happy anniversary,' the kids finally comply.
jokes.punchline: Their delivery is robotically performative and weirdly clinical — 'congratulations on your ongoing love for one another' and 'you did it!' — treating their parents' marriage like an endurance competition.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalating absurdity of their forced congratulations — 'ongoing love,' 'you did it' — perfectly captures the Rose children's emotional stunted-ness while being genuinely hilarious. 'You did it!' especially lands because it reframes marriage as a bizarre athletic feat. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's depressing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? They're being sweet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're treating love like an achievement to be congratulated. Next, they'll be asking us to show them our participation trophies.

jokes.text: 'You sound like the incestuous Bloomfields.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tells the kids 'that's enough' after their over-the-top anniversary wishes.
jokes.punchline: He calls them the 'incestuous Bloomfields' — upgrading Moira's earlier 'too friendly' and 'skinny dip' insinuations to a full explicit label.
jokes.explanation: A well-earned callback that escalates the Bloomfield joke to its logical extreme. 'Incestuous' is a funny word choice — blunt and surprising from the usually measured Johnny. The economy is exceptional: three words close out a multi-beat running joke perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, at least they're not as inappropriate as your mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, the kids were sweet. They tried.

jokes.text: Roland and Jocelyn crash the café with a dinner gift announcement, only to reveal 'we have a coupon.'
jokes.setup: Roland dramatically announces an exclusive dinner gift at 'the most exclusive eatery in Elmdale — very hard to get a table,' building it up as a generous and prestigious gesture.
jokes.punchline: 'We have a coupon. Four entrees for the price of three, it expires soon though.'
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland misdirection — the big generous gesture deflated by the coupon reveal. The specificity of 'four entrees for the price of three, it expires soon' makes it funnier. Perfectly in character for Roland, who is entirely oblivious to how this lands.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got something for you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very kind of you, Roland, but that's really not necessary.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you anyway.

jokes.text: 'That was a close call' — Roland and Jocelyn after nearly ruining the anniversary dinner plans.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn have just been gracefully turned down by the Roses for their anniversary dinner offer.
jokes.punchline: Roland congratulates himself — 'boy, that was a close call' — completely unaware that HE was the problem, not the coupon timing.
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-congratulatory obliviousness is a signature comedic trait. The line works because the audience knows he was the 'close call' all along. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got us a table at that fancy restaurant you've been wanting to go to. My treat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Really? That's very generous of you, Roland. What's the—wait, is this because you need something from me?

jokes.text: David discovers Stevie's yearbook and reads her voted superlative: 'Most likely to get the guy' — 'With that haircut?!'
jokes.setup: David finds Stevie's old yearbook and reads her superlative out loud.
jokes.punchline: 'Most likely to get the guy' followed immediately by David's withering 'With that haircut?!' — turning a compliment into an insult.
jokes.explanation: David's capacity for casual, reflexive shade is perfectly deployed here. 'With that haircut?!' is a strong punchline — short, punchy, and the question mark makes it funnier than an assertion. Character-specific and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've arranged a very exclusive dinner for you at the finest restaurant in town.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I pulled some strings, called in a favor. We're talking VIP treatment.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got a two-for-one coupon.

jokes.text: Stevie retorts: 'I'll have you know I was very popular with the boys. And girls, it seems.' — referencing David's own yearbook.
jokes.setup: Stevie defends her popularity after David mocks her yearbook photo.
jokes.punchline: 'And girls, it seems' — a dry callback implying David's yearbook reveals something about his own high school reputation with girls.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's comedy is built on perfectly timed, economical retorts. This one works by pivoting David's attack back onto him without raising her voice. The 'And girls, it seems' is an excellent deadpan button.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank God. I was dreading that dinner.

jokes.text: David's headshot got him a two-episode arc — on 'Dateline.' He played a boy abducted from a grocery store.
jokes.setup: David boasts that his headshot 'got me a two-episode arc on a TV series' — implying legitimate acting success.
jokes.punchline: 'Dateline.' He played a boy who got abducted from a grocery store — a true crime reenactment, not a scripted drama.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The misdirection is perfectly structured: 'two-episode arc on a TV series' builds genuine expectation, then 'Dateline' punctures it brilliantly — and 'boy abducted from a grocery store' is the cherry on top. It's very specific, deeply funny, and perfectly in keeping with the Rose family's absurd backstory. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You were popular in high school?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was actually pretty popular.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you look like that.

jokes.text: 'The performance was so riveting, people thought I actually was the Value-mart victim.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining his Dateline acting stint.
jokes.punchline: He's proud that people confused him with an actual crime victim — treating this as a compliment to his acting chops.
jokes.explanation: Escalates the Dateline joke wonderfully. 'Value-mart victim' is a hilarious specific detail — the budget-brand name adds texture. David's pride in being mistaken for a real abduction victim reveals his skewed sense of prestige.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm looking at your yearbook photo, and I can see why the boys were interested. Though I have to say, based on the number of signatures from girls saying 'You were so fun!' and 'Best summer ever!' with little hearts, I'm sensing your appeal may have been somewhat broader.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? That's just how people wrote in yearbooks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm. And yet, I'm not seeing this level of enthusiasm in the boys' section.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Bet that got you laid.' David: 'It did. It really did.'
jokes.setup: David has just finished boasting about his Dateline reenactment performance.
jokes.punchline: Stevie cuts to the absurd implication — that playing an abducted child made David attractive — and David confirms it without irony.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — six words deliver maximum absurdity. Stevie's casual conjecture is funny, but David's deadpan confirmation 'It did. It really did.' is the real gem. The pause implied between the two sentences makes it. Both characters are perfectly deployed. One of the episode's best exchanges.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, my headshot got me a television role.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Really? That's great.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, it was a crime reenactment show.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh. So not actual acting then.

jokes.text: David's telepathy joke: 'Are you telepathic or something, because I've been thinking about asking you that every day this week.'
jokes.setup: Stevie says she's surprised David hasn't asked her to leave early — framing it as a joke.
jokes.punchline: David immediately breaks the joke by responding with complete sincerity that he HAS been thinking about it every day, then backtracks with a weak 'I was just kidding.'
jokes.explanation: A solid bit of David-ness: he accidentally reveals his true feelings by taking the joke seriously, then fails to cover it. The 'Oh...no yeah, me too' from Stevie afterward is also funny. Good character-specific awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My performance was so convincing people thought I was the real victim.

jokes.text: Ted reveals there's a party at Mutt's, and Alexis didn't know she was invited — then asks what time the party is.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions 'the big soiree at Mutt's tonight' — assuming Alexis knows about it. She clearly does not.
jokes.punchline: After an awkward series of 'yeah, no, yeah, no' equivocations about whether she'd attend her ex's party, Alexis capitulates and asks: 'So um, what time is the party tonight?'
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed cringe beat. Alexis's 'yeah, no, yeah, no' spiral of self-justification followed by the immediate surrender is very true to her character. The joke earns its laugh through the sustained awkward buildup.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That was a really convincing performance.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. I was actually abducted by aliens.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, you weren't.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I wasn't. But it was a really effective way to have sex with people.

jokes.text: Yeah, no. Yeah, no, why wouldn't I be? / Um, were you invited? / Yeah. Yeah.
jokes.setup: David tactlessly points out Stevie might not have been invited to Mutt's party.
jokes.punchline: Stevie does the social-anxiety spiral of 'yeah no yeah no' — self-reassuring while clearly rattled — then David confirms he was invited, making it worse.
jokes.explanation: The 'yeah no yeah no' verbal tic is very Stevie — it captures her particular brand of defensive deflection. The beat where she confirms David was invited while she presumably wasn't is well-played cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, stay. No, actually, yes. Leave now.

jokes.text: Moira assumes John has something 'more grand' planned beyond the sweet anniversary gesture.
jokes.setup: Johnny presents a sweet anniversary moment; Moira receives it warmly but immediately recalibrates her expectations upward.
jokes.punchline: She compliments him on the 'precursor no doubt to something more grand' — revealing she's already treating his gesture as insufficient, while smiling.
jokes.explanation: A sharp Moira character moment — her entitled assumptions delivered as warmth is a signature comedic register for her. Doesn't quite land as a big laugh but effectively develops the anniversary storyline.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I've been thinking about what you said, and I really don't think it was that bad.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. I mean, yes, perhaps my tone was a bit off, but the content was—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You were terrible.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. You're... you're serious right now?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Very.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate your honesty, and I'm glad we could have this conversation, and I will certainly take what you've said into consideration moving forward.

jokes.text: Johnny reveals the 'surprise' restaurant — the exact one Roland and Jocelyn just told them about.
jokes.setup: Johnny builds up to revealing a 'surprise' restaurant booking for their anniversary, having clearly scrambled to arrange it last minute.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately identifies it as 'the one that Roland and Jocelyn just told us about' — deflating his pretense of having planned ahead.
jokes.explanation: A payoff to the coupon scene. The joke works on two levels: Johnny is caught improvising, and the callback to Roland's plot device is well-timed. The structure is clean.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So Mutt's throwing a party? That's... nice. I mean, I wasn't invited, obviously, because I have my own things going on. Very important things. But good for him. Parties are fine. I don't need to go to parties to validate my social standing or anything like that.

jokes.text: Moira describes the desired anniversary dinner as 'Lady and the Tramp meets 9½ Weeks kind of dinner date.'
jokes.setup: Moira confirms she wants an intimate romantic dinner for two.
jokes.punchline: Her specific cinematic references — the innocent romance of Lady and the Tramp crossed with the erotic thriller 9½ Weeks — is a perfectly Moira collision of registers.
jokes.explanation: A great Moira line. The mashup of Lady and the Tramp (a children's film) with 9½ Weeks (a softcore erotic thriller) is brilliantly absurd and reveals her theatrical, overwrought romantic sensibility. Only Moira Rose could produce this sentence. Highly quotable and very rewatchable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't possibly go to Mutt's party. I have a previous engagement that simply cannot be rescheduled. It's a matter of utmost importance.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Let me guess, you're washing your hair?

jokes.text: At the restaurant, Johnny confirms the reservation he 'of course made weeks ago' — visibly scrambling.
jokes.setup: Johnny is at the front desk, clearly having made the reservation just this afternoon after the Roland encounter.
jokes.punchline: 'We had a reservation which I of course made uh, weeks ago' — the verbal stumble mid-sentence ('uh') betrays the lie.
jokes.explanation: A functional in-character lie that gets a mild laugh from the obvious verbal stumble. The comedic concept isn't novel but the performance clearly sells it. Decent filler joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Happy anniversary. I got you flowers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Flowers? Johnny, these are the same flowers you get me every year.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You love flowers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I love *gestures* — I love grand romantic gestures. I love being surprised. I love feeling like you've put thought into something.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I did put thought into it. I thought, 'Moira loves flowers.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you thought wrong.

jokes.text: Moira deflects to complimenting Johnny: 'You always could swing a good table at the last minute, John.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just awkwardly confessed the reservation was last-minute.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots to a compliment that simultaneously acknowledges the lie and forgives it — the passive-aggressive grace note of 'at the last minute' confirms she knows but won't press.
jokes.explanation: A layered Moira moment — the compliment-as-acknowledgment structure is sharp. She's gracious and cutting simultaneously. Good character work delivered economically.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how romantic! A candlelit dinner. You know, it reminds me of that scene in Beauty and the Beast, except instead of Mrs. Potts pouring the tea, it's me pouring hot wax on your chest while you call me 'Mistress.'

jokes.text: The Roses run into their old friends Bev and Don at the restaurant — huge, theatrical recognition scene.
jokes.setup: The Roses are finally on their intimate anniversary dinner when old wealthy friends appear.
jokes.punchline: The theatrical over-the-top greeting ('Oh my God, the Roses! You're alive!') signals both relief that they survived financial ruin and the social performance of it — followed immediately by Moira inviting them to join.
jokes.explanation: 'You're alive!' is a funny encapsulation of how their rich friends viewed the Roses' financial collapse — as social death. It sets up the more pointed comedy later when Don makes Schitt's Creek jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's quite impressive, considering you called them this afternoon.

jokes.text: David discovers the fishy ice cubes made from well water at the party.
jokes.setup: David is about to use ice cubes in his drink at Mutt's party.
jokes.punchline: Jake warns him the ice cubes are made from well water and 'smell a little fishy' — a quietly absurd detail of rural life that David clearly hasn't encountered.
jokes.explanation: A subtle fish-out-of-water (literally) joke. The detail is specific and grounded — a real rural quirk — which gives it more comedic credibility than a made-up absurdity. Functional rather than standout.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that the Hendersons? I heard they lost everything in the market crash. How are they even alive?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, they're right there.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: I know, that's what I'm saying — it's remarkable.

jokes.text: Jake introduces himself: 'I'm Jake.' David: 'David. It's nice to meet you, handsome.'
jokes.setup: Jake and David meet for the first time at the party.
jokes.punchline: David casually drops 'handsome' into a standard introduction with zero hesitation — revealing his immediate interest while being completely unbothered about it.
jokes.explanation: David's breezy confidence in flirtation — completely at odds with his anxious neurosis in other areas — is a consistent and charming character trait. The economy here is excellent: 'handsome' is the entire joke in one word.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I wouldn't do that if I were you. Those ice cubes are from like three months ago.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: The ice maker's been broken since you got here. I've just been refreezing the same water.

jokes.text: Reaction beat: David's flustered exit after meeting Jake — 'I-I'll-I'm gonna... So I'll just...'
jokes.setup: David has just confidently called Jake 'handsome' on introduction.
jokes.punchline: He then immediately dissolves into stuttering incoherence trying to excuse himself — revealing his boldness was a bluff.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between David's confident opener and his flustered retreat is a perfectly observed character reversal. The transcript fragment of broken speech is visually funny even on the page.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm aware of the ice situation, thank you. When you're making cocktails with a blender that sounds like it's auditioning for a horror film, you take certain liberties with the ice cube situation.

jokes.text: Stevie arrives at the party dressed up: 'Wow. Look at you.' — 'I do what I want. And tonight I'm wanting to meet someone.'
jokes.setup: David expresses surprise at Stevie's dressed-up appearance at Mutt's party.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's defensive non-explanation — 'I do what I want' — followed by the unexpectedly earnest 'tonight I'm wanting to meet someone' is charming and funny.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's defense mechanism of projecting authority while being vulnerable is well-played here. 'I do what I want' as a deflection before the honest admission works well in her register.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: Well hello there, David Rose. I'm Jake. And I have to say, you are absolutely stunning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Thank you. I... that's very kind of you to say.

jokes.text: Stevie claims she has a 'certain high school reputation to live up to,' referencing her yearbook superlative.
jokes.setup: Stevie is at the party looking unusually put-together and optimistic about meeting someone.
jokes.punchline: She frames it as reclaiming her identity as someone who 'gets the guy' — a callback to her yearbook that gives her night out a quietly absurd justification.
jokes.explanation: A competent callback that tracks Stevie's character arc for the evening. Not especially funny on its own but works as connective tissue.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your hair looks different today.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I didn't wash it. I'm not going to apologize for that.

jokes.text: David tells Stevie the guy at the bar is 'into him,' then reveals it's Jake — the man he just met.
jokes.setup: David confidently tells Stevie he can tell when a guy is into him — then reveals it's Jake.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's reply: 'Why, was he one of your high school conquests?' — implying David's radar may just be familiarity bias.
jokes.explanation: A decent exchange that sets up the Jake competition subplot. Stevie's skepticism is in character but the joke itself is fairly routine.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, I can see both shores, so...

jokes.text: 'Having swam in both ponds, I can say that I might be able to navigate those waters a little better than you.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are competing over who will successfully attract Jake.
jokes.punchline: David argues his bisexuality gives him superior insight into male attraction — delivered with maximum pomposity using a mixed aquatic metaphor.
jokes.explanation: A fan-favorite line. David's breezy invocation of his bisexuality as a strategic advantage is perfectly in character — the pompous confidence, the slightly garbled metaphor ('swam in both ponds... navigate those waters'). The whole line is very quotable and very David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is that my bison?

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: Your bison? It's been here for years.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's a very distinctive bison.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: It's just a coat rack now, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A coat rack? That's a mounted bison!

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: It's holding coats.

jokes.text: Bev and Don telling the stuffed bison story — 'She's moved it to the front hall!'
jokes.setup: The wealthy friends are trading stories about their mutual rich acquaintance Izzy Tennison.
jokes.punchline: 'She's moved it to the front hall!' — the indignation about a stuffed bison's relocation is a perfect encapsulation of oblivious wealth.
jokes.explanation: The humor here is in the absurdity of wealthy people treating a stuffed bison's placement as a significant social event. It's more atmospheric than laugh-out-loud, setting up the class contrast that will pay off when John confronts Don later.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: So these grown wealthy men are crawling through the dog door at Izzy's house.

jokes.text: Don and Borden were caught crawling through the doggie door — 'Borden started it!'
jokes.setup: Bev is telling a story about a country house party.
jokes.punchline: Grown wealthy men are caught crawling through a doggie door, with Don immediately blaming Borden — the juvenile 'he started it' defense from an adult is the comic payoff.
jokes.explanation: A mildly funny rich-people-behaving-badly story. The doggie door image is good, but it's mostly setup for the atmosphere. The 'he started it' defense is a reliable but not original comic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need some time alone to think.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But you just said you wanted to spend the afternoon with us.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that was before I realized I needed to be alone. You see, when one reaches a certain level of sophistication, solitude becomes a necessity rather than a preference.

jokes.text: Oh, wish we could've been. / Maybe next time.
jokes.setup: Bev says they should've been there at the Tennison party.
jokes.punchline: The Roses' 'wish we could've been' and 'maybe next time' are poignant ironic statements — both know they almost certainly won't be invited, given how their fortunes have changed.
jokes.explanation: More cringe than comedy — the Roses performing normalcy while knowing they're excluded. The irony is present but the joke is gentle and mostly serves dramatic purpose.

dialogue.speaker: Tennessee
dialogue.line: Alexis, your hair is like a golden waterfall of sophistication cascading down your shoulders like a wealthy mermaid who won the lottery and decided to become a shampoo commercial.

jokes.text: Roland and Jocelyn appear at the same restaurant — 'we just ran into each other.'
jokes.setup: The Roses have escaped to their intimate anniversary dinner.
jokes.punchline: Roland and Jocelyn appear at the exact same restaurant — the third time the Schitts have intruded on the anniversary.
jokes.explanation: The running joke of the Schitts perpetually intruding on the Rose anniversary pays off here. Jocelyn's 'it's their anniversary, Roland, just let them do what they want' while Roland actively intrudes is a good irony beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A poem? I'm a poem. That's... that's sweet. A little poem. Not a big poem, just a little one. Like, a haiku-sized poem. A poem you'd find in a greeting card next to a picture of a cat in a tiny hat.

jokes.text: Jocelyn to Roland: 'It's their anniversary, Roland, just let them do what they want.' — immediately followed by Roland crashing the table.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is telling Roland not to intrude on the Roses' anniversary dinner.
jokes.punchline: The statement is made in the very act of intruding — 'just let them do what they want' precedes Roland asking to join them.
jokes.explanation: The self-contradiction within a single conversational exchange is well-timed. Jocelyn's total lack of self-awareness — or perhaps her awareness that Roland can't be stopped — makes this work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're like a... a gazelle. But like, a smart gazelle. With better hair. And you smell like expensive cologne, which is good because some people smell like cheap cologne, which is just sad. But you don't. You smell like... confidence? Is that a smell? Anyway, you're great.

jokes.text: Johnny's barely suppressed resignation: 'Let's all sit at one table.'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira's anniversary dinner has been invaded by old friends and now the Schitts.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat, defeated 'let's uh... let's all sit at one table' — the pause and verbal stumble conveying absolute resignation — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Eugene Levy is perfect at this kind of beaten-down dignity comedy. The verbal stutter before the acceptance conveys everything. Good reaction comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry, what was your name again?

dialogue.speaker: Tennessee
dialogue.line: Tennessee.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, Tennessee. That's such a... unique name.

dialogue.speaker: Tennessee
dialogue.line: Thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, it's really lucky you're as pretty as you are, because that name is absolutely ridiculous.

jokes.text: Alexis meets Mutt's girlfriend Tennessee and calls her 'Tallahassee.'
jokes.setup: Alexis meets Mutt's new girlfriend, who has the distinctive name Tennessee.
jokes.punchline: When Alexis says goodbye she calls her 'Tallahassee' — close enough to show she wasn't paying attention but specific enough to suggest it was a power move.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant Alexis Rose moment. 'Tallahassee' instead of 'Tennessee' walks a perfect ambiguity: was it genuine forgetfulness or a calculated slight? Either reading is character-true and funny. The follow-up compliment — 'I love that name, it's so unique' — adds another layer of either obliviousness or cruelty, making this a standout bit.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we're currently residing in a charming boutique establishment. It's very... intimate. The rooms are quite compact, which is very European. And the amenities are, shall we say, rustic in their authenticity. There's a wonderful homogeneity to the decor—everything matches because it's all the same, you see. The plumbing has character. Real character. You can hear it singing at all hours.

jokes.text: 'Thank God you look the way you do, because that name on the wrong girl would just... Ooh! So you should feel really good about yourself.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just called Tennessee 'Tallahassee' and is trying to compliment her way out of it.
jokes.punchline: She doubles down with a backhanded compliment implying the name is inherently unfortunate — then leaves Tennessee to parse whether she was insulted.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character comedy moments. Alexis's delivery of a compliment so backhanded it becomes an insult — 'that name on the wrong girl would just... Ooh!' — is a masterclass in her register. The trailing 'Ooh!' with its implied horror is perfect. You should feel really good about yourself as a button is chef's kiss. Deeply character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We have 8 rooms available.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, when I describe it to people, I usually say we have closer to 12.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We have 8 rooms.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, but 12 sounds better.

jokes.text: It was so nice to finally meet you, Tallahassee.
jokes.setup: Alexis is saying goodbye to Tennessee after a tense introduction.
jokes.punchline: She gets Tennessee's name wrong — calling her 'Tallahassee' — which reads as either passive-aggressive or genuine Alexis-obliviousness (both are funny).
jokes.explanation: The name confusion is perfectly ambiguous — is Alexis being petty or is she just genuinely bad with names? The show lets it be both, which is stronger. 'Tallahassee' is also funnier than any other wrong answer would be.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course, we have an extensive staff to maintain the property.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, quite a large operation.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: How many people work for you?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, there's... one.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And he's adequate.

jokes.text: Roland interrupting the emotional reunion: 'You guys haven't ordered yet, have you? Because I need to present this coupon to the waiter first.'
jokes.setup: The Roses are in the middle of an emotional reconnection with their old friends.
jokes.punchline: Roland cuts through the sentiment with urgent coupon logistics — the mundane interrupting the profound.
jokes.explanation: Roland's coupon anxiety paying off as a scene-disrupting joke is excellent. The timing — right in the middle of emotional social theater — is perfect. His complete inability to read the room is played with perfect consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: We drove through this town once. Worst place you've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: Bev
dialogue.line: Absolute dump. Nothing there. Who would ever live there?

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: I mean, what kind of person settles in a place like that?

jokes.text: Johnny's reaction to the coupon: 'Don't know what that means.' — Jocelyn: 'He's so ironic, this one.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just demanded to present his coupon to the waiter first.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat 'Don't know what that means' — delivered to the table — prompts Jocelyn to mistake his genuine exasperation for ironic humor.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's misreading of Johnny's deadpan sincerity as sophistication is a great joke about social class perception. It's also self-aware meta-comedy: the Schitts are now interpreting the Roses' frustration as stylish irony.

jokes.text: Moira describing their home: 'A rustic, ranch style... bungalow... that... It's very nice.' — John adds: 'Very nice, it's got plenty of parking, and a TV in every room.'
jokes.setup: Don and Bev ask where the Roses are living now, clearly expecting a lavish answer.
jokes.punchline: Moira trails off searching for positive spin — 'bungalow... that... It's very nice' — and Johnny's best endorsement is 'plenty of parking and a TV in every room' — the kind of selling points one finds in a roadside motel.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed cringe beat. The Rose family's attempts to spin their motel living to their wealthy friends is painfully funny. 'Plenty of parking and a TV in every room' is a motel brochure description, which is exactly where they're living.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: Schittsville? What kind of name is Schittsville?

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: It looks like someone sneezed out a town.

jokes.text: Bev: 'That must be a nightmare to clean!' — Moira: 'Um, Stevie does an adequate job.' — Johnny: 'Stevie's the only staff.'
jokes.setup: Bev imagines the scale of their supposed ten-bedroom home requires extensive staff.
jokes.punchline: Moira's performative 'Stevie does an adequate job' — implying multiple staff — is immediately deflated by Johnny's honest 'Stevie's the only staff,' and of course Stevie is the motel clerk.
jokes.explanation: The Moira/Johnny double act here is well done. Moira builds the illusion; Johnny punctures it. The rhythm of the exchange is tight and the punchline (Stevie = motel clerk, not household staff) lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You seemed so cool after we broke up.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was crying so much it frightened the animals at the vet clinic.

jokes.text: This olive tapenade is... / Salty, I know! / Is it? I-I thought it was good, but yeah, it's salty.
jokes.setup: John starts to comment on the olive tapenade at dinner.
jokes.punchline: Moira reflexively interrupts with a self-deprecating 'Salty, I know!' — she's so used to damage control that she preempts a complaint that wasn't coming.
jokes.explanation: A small, charming joke. Moira's anxiety about the evening manifests as automatic self-criticism, cutting off a compliment mid-sentence. John's confused 'Is it? I-I thought it was good' is the perfect innocent straight-man response.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I went to this couples resort alone, and I ended up getting life advice from a zip line instructor.

jokes.text: Moira cuts off the awkward staff conversation by commenting on the olive tapenade: 'This olive tapenade is—' — Bev: 'Salty, I know!'
jokes.setup: The conversation about staff is becoming uncomfortably revealing.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots abruptly to the food — 'This olive tapenade is—' — only to have Bev finish her sentence with 'Salty, I know!' implying even the redirect fails.
jokes.explanation: A small but functional cringe beat. Moira's retreat into food commentary as a social escape is a recognizable rich-person gambit. The salty tapenade becoming a mini-complaint is a minor pay-off.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: David? What are you doing here alone?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm eating my feelings. Which, given my current emotional state, explains the portion size.

jokes.text: Don tells the 'Schittsville' story — the town was 'living up to its name!'
jokes.setup: Don mentions driving through a town called 'Schittsville' (he's misremembering 'Schitt's Creek') and it looked like a dump.
jokes.punchline: 'From the looks of it, the town was living up to its name!' — Don is unknowingly insulting the town where the Roses live, in front of the Roses.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony — Don unknowingly mocking the Roses' home — sets up Johnny's confrontation. The joke itself is a bit easy (the town name joke has been the show's premise from the start), but the social tension it creates is valuable.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: Do you shame eat?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where would I even start?

jokes.text: Johnny's attempted protest — 'Let's not go for the low-hanging fruit here, Don.' — Roland immediately endorses Don: 'Please continue, Don. You see, Roland gets it!'
jokes.setup: Don is telling Schittsville jokes at the dinner table.
jokes.punchline: Johnny tries to shut it down with dignity; Roland enthusiastically encourages more — hilariously oblivious that these are jokes about his town — making Johnny's moral authority collapse.
jokes.explanation: Roland accidentally undermining Johnny's moral stance is sharp. 'You see, Roland gets it!' from Don — as if Roland is a sophisticated appreciator of irony rather than someone who doesn't recognize the insult — is a well-layered comic moment.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: This wine is absolutely undrinkable. The nose is all wrong, the finish is thin, and the body is completely absent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I just pick the one with the highest alcohol content. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: Alexis admits she started drinking before the party with 'all four of those peach schnapps you left in my fridge.'
jokes.setup: Ted asks how Alexis is so relaxed given the circumstances (attending her ex Mutt's party).
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals she pre-gamed with four peach schnapps — a very specific, very unglamorous drink choice for someone who presents herself as sophisticated.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'peach schnapps' does the heavy lifting. It's a distinctly non-glamorous drink for Alexis, which subverts her usual affectations. 'All four of them' confirms quantity without apology.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: This wine is absolutely ghastly. The nose is nonexistent, the palate is thin, and the finish is abrupt.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, it gets you drunk, doesn't it?

jokes.text: Mutt admits he 'couldn't stop crying' after the breakup and 'was scaring all the animals at work.'
jokes.setup: Alexis was surprised that Mutt seemed 'so cool' about their breakup.
jokes.punchline: Mutt's revelation that he was secretly devastated — phrased as 'I was scaring all the animals at work' — is funny because the animals-at-work detail is so specific and unexpectedly literal.
jokes.explanation: The animals detail is perfect — it takes a standard 'I was heartbroken' confession and makes it specific and visual. Mutt works with animals, so the detail is character-grounded, but the image of him sobbing so hard he frightened livestock is genuinely funny and touching simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about our friendship, and I realize it's completely conditional. The moment things got difficult, you disappeared.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not fair. I was there for you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Were you? Or were you just there when it was convenient? When it made you look good?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're being ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Am I? Name one time you went out of your way for me when there was nothing in it for you.

jokes.text: Mutt went on their honeymoon alone — 'the zip line instructor at Los Passions Couples Resort told me I just gotta get through it' — meaning his fear of heights.
jokes.setup: Mutt describes going on their planned honeymoon without Alexis and finding comfort from a zip line instructor's advice.
jokes.punchline: The advice 'you just gotta get through it' is revealed to mean his fear of heights — not the heartbreak — which he then clarifies with 'but with you, it just took a little longer.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structurally constructed joke. The misdirection is elegant: the zip line instructor's advice sounds like it will be a heartfelt life lesson, then the pivot — 'meaning my fear of heights, which I did get through' — pulls the rug out. Then 'but with you, it just took a little longer' restores the emotional weight without sacrificing the humor. Funny and poignant simultaneously. Exceptional structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Roland and Jocelyn have been nothing but kind to us since we arrived in this town. When our friends from the city heard about our situation, they scattered like rats. But these people—they've opened their homes, their hearts. I won't have them disrespected.

jokes.text: 'Staff dance?' — 'Yes!'
jokes.setup: An awkward emotional moment between Alexis and Mutt after his heartbreak confession.
jokes.punchline: One of them suggests a 'staff dance' — a private callback to whatever dance they developed as staff at the resort — as an escape from emotion, and the other immediately accepts.
jokes.explanation: The economy is striking — two words deflect a heavy emotional scene into a charming absurdist beat. The audience's curiosity about what the 'staff dance' is adds to the delight. Sweet and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that was very generous of you to pick up the bill.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, I didn't pick up the bill. I just didn't pay my half.

jokes.text: David is 'shame eating in private' at the party.
jokes.setup: David is caught eating alone at the party.
jokes.punchline: He describes it as 'shame eating in private' — a perfectly specific and relatable concept delivered with complete David Rose unapologetic-ness.
jokes.explanation: 'Shame eating in private' is an exceptional comic coinage. The concept is immediately recognizable; the specific phrasing is both precise and funny. David's complete lack of embarrassment about confessing it to a stranger adds the character-comedy layer. Highly quotable — this kind of phrase becomes part of a show's lexicon.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, we actually live here now.

jokes.text: Jake asks 'What is shame eating?' — David: 'I don't think we have enough time for that tonight.'
jokes.setup: Jake is confused by the phrase 'shame eating in private.'
jokes.punchline: David declines to explain it — implying the concept is so complex or the history so extensive that a brief party conversation cannot do it justice.
jokes.explanation: The follow-up to 'shame eating' is equally strong. 'I don't think we have enough time for that tonight' is perfectly deadpan — it reframes David's shame eating not as a one-time event but as a sprawling personal phenomenon. Great economy, great character work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I did woodworking in high school.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I witnessed a horrific accident in woodworking class.

jokes.text: David's awkward 'not here with' clarification about Stevie: 'No, we're not together, I'm very much single, so.'
jokes.setup: Jake asks if David is there with Stevie; David says yes
jokes.punchline: David immediately over-clarifies that they're not together and he's 'very much single' — the desperation of the clarification is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Very much single, so' — the 'so' dangling at the end is doing comedic work, implying 'so... you know... just putting that out there.' Good character beat but not the sharpest joke in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just think it's important to establish a baseline of emotional availability before we engage in any sort of physical intimacy. I have a very specific skincare routine that takes about forty-five minutes, and I need to know if you're comfortable with that level of commitment.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: Do you want to get dinner?

jokes.text: 'Who do you have to kill around here to get another bottle of this undrinkable wine?'
jokes.setup: The dinner table has become increasingly awkward with Don and Bev's oblivious wealth-talk.
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical hyperbole — asking who needs to be killed for more of a wine she's already called 'undrinkable' — encapsulates her coping mechanism: theatrical excess.
jokes.explanation: A classic Moira Rose line — the self-contradictory request (she calls it undrinkable but demands more) delivered with maximum dramatic flair. The hyperbolic 'who do you have to kill' elevates it above a standard complaint.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where were you?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was at this really sketchy part of town, and I didn't want to offend any of the drug dealers, so I just smiled and waved at everyone.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I think it's good.' — then: 'I ordered the one with the highest alcohol content.'
jokes.setup: Moira calls the wine 'undrinkable.'
jokes.punchline: Roland defends it — 'I think it's good' — then reveals his selection criterion was purely alcohol volume, not quality.
jokes.explanation: Roland's wine connoisseurship being purely transactional — 'highest alcohol content' — is a perfect character detail. The deadpan sincerity makes it funnier than if he were self-aware about it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, Jake is into you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, he looked at me a certain way.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What way?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A way that suggested he wanted to do things to me. Specific things. Crude things.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Did he say any words?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, but his eyes were very loud.

jokes.text: Don's class-based job question to Roland: 'What do you do, Roland? Something in agriculture, by the looks of it?'
jokes.setup: The class dynamics at the table are already tense; Don assumes Roland's appearance indicates he's a farmhand
jokes.punchline: Roland is, in fact, the mayor — which makes Don's condescension instantly ironic and reveals Don's snobbery
jokes.explanation: The setup is good — 'by the looks of it' is the perfect snobbish qualifier. The payoff (mayor) flips the hierarchy cleanly. Sets up the episode's emotional climax well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I kissed Jake Champ.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. He told me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No, he didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He did. He said it was nice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, he's lying.

jokes.text: After learning Roland is the mayor, Don asks: 'Not Schittsville, I hope! What do you even call someone from Schittsville? A Schitter? A Schitthead?'
jokes.setup: Don has been making Schitt's Creek-adjacent jokes all dinner; he now has Roland in front of him
jokes.punchline: Don escalates his crude wordplay, unaware (or uncaring) that Roland is literally from the town being mocked
jokes.explanation: The joke itself is crude wordplay at the low end of craft — 'Schitthead' is a cheap pun. Its function in the episode is primarily dramatic (pushing Johnny toward his speech) rather than comedic. The humor comes from Don's obliviousness more than the wordplay itself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Jake likes me more than you. I can prove it.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: How?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll ask him who he'd rather have go down on him.

jokes.text: Johnny's speech: 'I'm sitting here in a half decent restaurant with my wife and our friends, and all you two have done is complain about the food and pretend you didn't leave us high and dry after we lost everything.'
jokes.setup: Don is making Schitt's Creek jokes at the table; Johnny has reached his limit.
jokes.punchline: The speech culminates in 'a half decent restaurant' — a quietly devastating self-aware qualifier. The Rose patriarch defending his dignity using the modest adjective 'half decent' to describe a restaurant he scrambled to book that afternoon.
jokes.explanation: This is more dramatic than comic, but the 'half decent restaurant' self-description is a genuinely funny moment within an otherwise serious speech. John Rose's dignity recalibration — from 'the best restaurants in the world' to 'half decent' — is both comedic and moving. The scene as a whole earns this through character work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you guys high right now?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Alexis, keep your voice down.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're high! You're both high!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We are not high. We just... we had some of Mutt's special brownies, and we may have miscalculated the dosage.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, please. Your mother is having a very important conversation with a squirrel right now, and I would appreciate it if you didn't draw attention to us.

jokes.text: Johnny's speech praising Roland and Jocelyn: 'Roland and Jocelyn here could not have been more generous with what little they have. They found us a place to live, they've offered us their truck whenever we've needed it, they've invited us to their parties, they even offered to take us out to dinner tonight.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is delivering a heartfelt defense of Roland and Jocelyn against Don's class snobbery
jokes.punchline: The list of Roland and Jocelyn's generosities inadvertently reads as a list of things a person of means would never need — truck, motel, coupon dinner — which is poignant and funny simultaneously
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most earned moments. The comedy is embedded in the gap between what these gestures mean (enormous, to a family who has nothing) and how they sound to Don and Bev (hilariously modest). 'What little they have' and the coupon callback at the end are perfectly constructed. The joke functions as comedy AND the episode's emotional thesis. High earned-vs-cheap score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're going to have a family bonding experience. We're going to dance together.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, no.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes. Come on, get up. Let's dance as a family.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not dancing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You are all going to dance with me right now, and you're going to like it.

jokes.text: Roland interrupts Johnny's heartfelt speech to clarify: 'Just to be clear, Johnny, we were always gonna split the bill, it's just with the coupon...'
jokes.setup: Johnny is mid-monologue, defending the Schitts' generosity to Don and Bev — praising how they 'offered to take them out to dinner tonight.'
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately undercuts the compliment by clarifying the financial arrangement — breaking the emotional momentum of the speech with coupon logistics for the third time.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke by a significant margin. The coupon callback has been seeded three times; here it pays off in the most dramatically incongruous possible moment. Johnny is delivering what should be a moving, cathartic speech — and Roland's inability to let the coupon thing go derails it perfectly. The structure (build-build-subvert during the most serious scene) is exceptional. Roland's obliviousness isn't played for cruelty; it's pure character. Deserves comparison to the best callback work in the series.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You are the most extraordinary man. Your presence in my life has fundamentally altered the trajectory of my existence in ways that defy articulation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, just say 'I love you.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I love you.

jokes.text: 'And that town you passed through — it's not called Schittsville. It's called Schitt's Creek. And it's where we live.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has defended his new community to Don and Bev, who have been mocking Schitt's Creek all evening.
jokes.punchline: The correction and proud ownership — 'it's where we live' — delivered flatly after such a buildup, is both the joke and the emotional payoff of the episode.
jokes.explanation: The season finale's emotional and comedic peak. The correction 'it's not called Schittsville, it's called Schitt's Creek' has inherent comedy in the name itself, but 'and it's where we live' transforms it. Johnny's unashamed ownership of the town the show is named for is the perfect distillation of the show's thesis — dignity through acceptance. The joke and the heart land simultaneously.

jokes.text: Stevie/David competition over Jake — 'Do you drink red wine or white wine?' — Jake: 'I'm more of a beer guy.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is attempting to win Jake's attention using her 'most likely to get the guy' skills.
jokes.punchline: Her opening gambit — an oddly formal 'do you drink red or white wine?' — is immediately deflated by Jake's 'I'm more of a beer guy.'
jokes.explanation: The wine opener as a flirtation gambit is itself a bit stiff, which is the joke — Stevie's approach is oddly formal for a casual party. Jake deflating it with a simple preference is functional comedy.

jokes.text: David's woodworking anecdote: 'Christie McFadden accidentally nailed her hand to her paper towel holder, so the lesson is just not to nail stuff...'
jokes.setup: Jake mentions he makes furniture in a wood shop; David tries to bond over woodworking experience.
jokes.punchline: His high school woodworking story ends with someone nailing their hand to a paper towel holder — a very specific injury — and the lesson David draws is the spectacularly vague 'just not to nail stuff.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating specificity (Christie McFadden, paper towel holder) colliding with the vague punchline ('just not to nail stuff') is a good structural joke. David's earnest attempt to connect with a craftsman through a disaster story is very much in character.

jokes.text: Oh, you work retail as well? / No, no. The wood shop. I make furniture.
jokes.setup: Jake mentions he has to get to the shop early — Stevie assumes retail.
jokes.punchline: Jake is a furniture maker — a pleasantly surprising and tactile answer that Stevie clearly hadn't expected.
jokes.explanation: Mild character-establishing humor. The gap between Stevie's assumption (retail) and reality (artisan woodworker) is a gentle subversion. Primarily expository.

jokes.text: Jake: 'I'm a pretty open-minded guy. So why don't you stop by the shop sometime?'
jokes.setup: David has just rambled through an embarrassing woodworking anecdote — apparently not repelling Jake.
jokes.punchline: Jake's 'I'm a pretty open-minded guy' followed by an invitation functions as a coded signal of interest — the open-mindedness referring to more than carpentry tolerance.
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'open-minded' lands cleanly. The ambiguity — is this a friendship invitation or flirtation? — serves the upcoming competition between David and Stevie.

jokes.text: David's triumphant 'I knew it!' reaction after Jake leaves.
jokes.setup: Jake has just extended a coded invitation to David.
jokes.punchline: David's immediate 'I knew it!' — vindication of his 'both ponds' radar claim — is delivered with maximum self-satisfaction.
jokes.explanation: Three words paying off the earlier both-ponds boast. Economy is excellent. The audience has been waiting for this and it doesn't disappoint.

jokes.text: The 'townies were trying to get us high and we didn't want to offend them because they're doing so well in life.'
jokes.setup: Alexis and Ted have disappeared from the party for a while and are questioned about where they were.
jokes.punchline: The excuse — they couldn't refuse the townies' drugs for fear of insulting them — treats accepting marijuana as social courtesy toward people doing 'so well in life' (they're townies, by definition not wealthy).
jokes.explanation: A superb Alexis Rose joke. The class condescension is wrapped in performed deference — she got high because she was being polite to people she's clearly looking down on. 'They're doing so well in life' as a justification for tolerating their presence is perfectly Alexis.

jokes.text: 'Actually, those boys did burn a bridge last summer. Luckily they don't get as bored as they used to.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says she didn't want to 'burn a bridge' by refusing the townies' drugs.
jokes.punchline: Mutt takes 'burn a bridge' literally and confirms the townies actually did burn a bridge last summer — treating arson as a charming local color detail, not a crisis.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay joke. The literal/figurative pivot on 'burn a bridge' is perfectly executed, and the follow-up — 'luckily they don't get as bored as they used to' — adds a layer of absurdist dark comedy suggesting the arson was a regular occurrence. Mutt's casual delivery of this information is the final touch. One of the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Stevie reveals she kissed Jake: 'I kissed him too.' — David: 'Well, that didn't happen.' Stevie: 'Who's to say?'
jokes.setup: David announces he 'necked' Jake behind the barn and asks Stevie for validation.
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually reveals she also kissed Jake — upending David's victory lap — and his denial 'well, that didn't happen' is met with Stevie's perfectly dry 'who's to say?'
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the David/Stevie Jake competition is excellent. 'Who's to say?' is a perfectly calibrated Stevie response — not a boast, not a denial, just an infuriating philosophical shrug. David's sputtering reaction is implied. The discovery that Jake kissed both of them (and presumably didn't tell either) sets up a great next beat.

jokes.text: So Jake... is into me. / Is he? / Yeah! / What makes you think that? / Because we just necked out behind the barn.
jokes.setup: Stevie announces her success with Jake; David is skeptical.
jokes.punchline: Physical evidence: they literally made out behind the barn. David's response will be everything.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's triumph and David's disbelief is well-played setup for the escalating Jake revelation that follows. 'Necked' is a deliberately old-fashioned word that adds charm.

jokes.text: David: 'When would you have had time to do that?' — Stevie: 'Did he invite you to his wood shop? Sorry, that came out wrong.'
jokes.setup: David is interrogating Stevie about when she kissed Jake.
jokes.punchline: Stevie asks 'did he invite you to his wood shop?' — a question that sounds like innuendo — then immediately says 'sorry, that came out wrong,' which only makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The wood shop innuendo joke is well-constructed. The genuine question (did Jake invite David to visit) sounds like a double entendre, and Stevie's self-correction 'sorry, that came out wrong' only deepens the implication rather than resolving it. The meta-awareness of the accidental pun is executed perfectly.

jokes.text: 'Smells like grass in here.' — 'Oh my God!' 'Oh my God!'
jokes.setup: The Rose family reunites at the motel. Johnny and Moira return from their disaster anniversary dinner.
jokes.punchline: Johnny observes 'smells like grass in here' — the parents are oblivious that their kids have been smoking marijuana — and the kids' panicked reactions reveal the truth.
jokes.explanation: A classic parental obliviousness joke. Not especially original as a comedic concept, but the timing and the kids' reaction beats carry it. The double 'Oh my God!' from both kids suggests they're genuinely panicked, which is funny given how unflappable they usually are.

jokes.text: Johnny's 'Okay, this is not okay! What are you guys doing here?!' — Moira: 'Shhhh! For once in your life, Shhhh!'
jokes.setup: The kids react with horror to their parents appearing at the party (or motel), seemingly having been caught.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'for once in your life, Shhhh!' is directed at whoever is speaking — likely meaning the family drama, which she silences to deliver something more important.
jokes.explanation: Moira overriding the chaos with her shushing is characteristic. The 'for once in your life' implies this is a long-standing issue. Works as setup for the family dance scene.

jokes.text: Johnny announces 'Tonight we are dancing as a family.' — David: 'What is happening?'
jokes.setup: The family has reunited at the motel room — apparently slightly high, slightly drunk, and slightly emotionally overwhelmed.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's completely sincere declaration that they are dancing as a family — apparently non-negotiable — followed by David's bewildered 'What is happening?' captures the show's sweet absurdism perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The family dance scene is the episode's emotional finale, but Johnny's announcement is funny in its sheer authority — 'I don't care what snarky remarks you kids are gonna make, we are dancing as a family.' David's 'what is happening?' is the perfect audience surrogate reaction. Character integration is exceptional for all four Roses.

jokes.text: Moira's speech: 'Do you know your father and I hold great affection for the two of you? It may not always be obvious, but—' — Johnny: 'Just say it, Moira!'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to express love to her children during the family dance — characteristically unable to say the word directly.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's exasperated 'Just say it, Moira!' — cutting through her performative circumlocution — forces the most direct expression of love Moira can manage.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most character-specific comedy moments. Moira's inability to say 'I love you' without elaborate prelude is a deeply established character trait. Johnny's 'Just say it, Moira!' in the middle of a family dance is funny and tender simultaneously. The economy of 'just say it' — five syllables that cut through all her theatrical apparatus — is excellent writing.

jokes.text: After Moira finally says 'We love you both very much' — David and Alexis respond with flat, unemotional 'Love you too' in unison.
jokes.setup: Moira has finally said 'we love you both very much' after being pushed by Johnny.
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis respond with completely flat, unemotional 'Love you too' — as if confirming a receipt rather than reciprocating parental love. But the fact they say it at all is the emotional payoff.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke in terms of structural and thematic payoff. This directly mirrors the opening scene where David asked 'what would you like us to say?' — forced, performative affection. Here, the affection is still delivered robotically, but it's real. The joke IS the emotional resolution of the episode: these two emotionally stunted children can only say 'I love you' in a flat monotone, but they say it. It's funny and devastating and exactly right. Maximum economy — four syllables each. The rewatch bonus is extremely high because the opening scene makes this retroactively perfect.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira go out for their anniversary and run into a couple they knew from their former life, while Alexis and David attend Mutt’s annual barn party, making up with some friends, and making out with others.
insight: Season finale leans on character cringe over punchlines, hitting just 3 jokes per minute.
